text: EXPERIMENTAL 5 Mw THERMAL CONVECTION MOLTEN SALT REACTOR
A preliminary study has been made of an experimental 5 Mw thermal convection molten salt reactor.
This reactor can be converted, after a period of low power operation, to a 50 Mw pilot power plant by adding a fuel pump, a larger sodium pump, and a turbo generator with associated equipment.
By adding a fuel pump to the 5 Mw reactor, it is possible to increase the capacity of the fuel system from 5 to 50 Mw.
1 shows an elevation of the reactor plant; Fig.
The dimensions and operating conditions for 5 and $50\mathrm{Mw}$ operation are given in Table I.
The reactor is five feet in diameter with a 6 inch thick blanket surrounding the core (see Appendix A).
The 5 MW reactor is inherently simple and reliable and requires no development of components.

In order to provide for future 50 Mw operation of the system, it is necessary that the fuel expansion tank be so designed that a sump type fuel pump can be installed in it and the sodium lines leading to the heat exchanger be sized to handle the flow required for 50 Mw operation.
The 5 Mw reactor would serve the following purposes:
Conversion to 50 Mw operation
When the above has been accomplished, the system can be converted to 50 Mw operation and operated as a pilot power plant.
Conversion to 50 Mw Operation (continued)

The fuel to sodium heat exchanger designed for the 5 Mw operation would be satisfactory for 50 Mw operation, because of the reduction in the fuel film resistance in going from laminar flow at the lower power to turbulent flow at the higher power.
If this turns out to be impractical, a blanket pump can be installed for 50 Mw operation.
1 and 2 and a new system consisting of a 10,000 gpm pump and sodium to steam heat exchanger is installed.
Table II shows a rough cost estimate, of approximately $10,000,000 for the construction of the 5 Mw plant.
For approximately $10,000,000 additional, this plant could be converted to the 50 Mw size, as shown in Table III.
No attempt was made in this limited study to optimize either the 5 or 50 Mw plants.
The 5 and 50 Mw power levels were chosen arbitrarily and it is quite possible that a different choice is preferable.
Among these are: 1) the design of the blanket circuit, 2) the method of removing fission gas, 3) the design of the 5 Mw heat dump, and 4) the design of the steam system.

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: The design variables in the Reference Design Reactor, described in reference 1, which it is desirable to match in a test reactor are, in order of their importance:

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: Using RDR-1 data for a basis, an eight foot core with $1\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ will go critical at a U-235 concentration of 12.5 x $10^{19}$ atoms/cc.

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: This concentration will render a five foot core critical when no thorium is present, and the critical mass will be about 85 kg.

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: A concentration of 36 x $10^{19}$ atoms/cc would make a four foot core critical, with a critical mass of about 130 kg.

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: In the case of a six inch blanket on a five foot core, if the core vessel failed and the system were operated as a one region reactor, the critical concentration would fall to about $4 \times 10^{19}$ , and the critical mass would be about $50 \, \text{kg}$ .

section: Selection of Uranium Concentration and Core Size
text: Adding about $3/8$ mole per cent thorium would raise the critical concentration back to $12 \times 10^{19}$ , and the critical mass to $140 \, \text{kg}$ .
Kinyon, "A Molten Salt Natural Convection Reactor System", ORNL 58-2-46
